overview: Serpentine Curve is a U/R tempo-control archetype that seeks to dictate the pace of the game through efficient interaction, card advantage, and escalating threats. It wins by out-interacting aggressive and combo decks early, then closing out midrange and control games with Lorien Revealed or a fully cast Serpentine Curve.
win_conditions: Lorien Revealed is your primary win condition
protect it with counterspells or discard.
A fully cast Serpentine Curve can also close out games if the board is open.
Use card draw to ensure you have enough resources to resolve your finisher.

keep_criteria: Keep hands with 2-3 lands, at least one Counterspell, and a turn-1 or turn-2 play (cantrip, cheap threat, or Serpentine Curve).
mulligan_criteria: Mulligan on 3+ lands with no early plays, hands with only Counterspell and no threats, or hands missing dual lands if your curve requires specific colors early.

early_game: Turn 1: Play a dual land (if possible), cast a cantrip or 1-drop to set up the curve.
Turn 2: Curve into a 2-drop threat or hold up Counterspell for key spells.
Land sequencing: Prioritize fetch/shock lands for fixing, then basics to avoid tapping on critical turns.
Always leave mana open for interaction unless you're committing to a curve play.
mid_game: Treat Counterspell as a tempo tool, not a panic button.
Use cantrips to maintain hand size and avoid flooding.
Never tap out for a Serpentine Curve unless you have protection or the board is clear.
Manage your graveyard carefully if Lorien Revealed interacts with it.
Commit threats when you have at least one counterspell or removal left.
Hold back if the opponent has untapped mana and likely holds a sweeper or heavy interaction.
Trade efficiently: use a counterspell on a game-ending threat, not a tempo play.
late_game: Lorien Revealed is your primary win condition
protect it with counterspells or discard.
A fully cast Serpentine Curve can also close out games if the board is open.
Use card draw to ensure you have enough resources to resolve your finisher.
Go all-in when the opponent has no interaction left, or when you can protect your finisher with a counterspell or discard.
Commit to Lorien Revealed if you have card advantage and they lack removal.

sequencing_tips: Common mistake: Overusing Counterspell on tempo plays instead of game-ending threats.
Unique interaction: Lorien Revealed’s recursion engine synergizes with Serpentine Curve’s escalating cost
cast Curve early to build graveyard value, then recur it later.
Always track opponent’s hand size and mana
bluffing is less effective in this format due to high interaction.
Practice mulligan decisions rigorously—this deck is highly sensitive to early game quality.

matchups.advice: Use cantrips to dig through counterspells, cast threats in response to their counterspells, or overload with multiple threats. Sideboard in spells that can’t be countered or that resolve partially.
Apply early pressure and use Counterspell on their ramp spells. Bring in artifact/enchantment hate and sweepers. Don’t let them get ahead; stabilize with efficient threats and card draw.

Prioritize early Counterspell and spot removal. Stabilize by trading efficiently and curving into threats that can outpace their aggression. Don’t overcommit to big spells; focus on survival and card advantage.
Pressure with threats and use card draw to out-value them. Sideboard in finishers and hate on their interaction. Force them to use resources on your threats, then close out with Lorien Revealed.

Win the interaction war by holding up counterspells for key spells. Trade efficiently and use card draw to out-resource them. Sideboard in hate for their finishers and prioritize board presence over interaction.

common_threats: Watch for sweepers (especially in Jund Wildfire and Blue Terror) and ramp spells (Elves, Grixis Affinity).
Note when opponents hold up mana past turn 3—they likely have interaction.
Track their graveyard if Jund Wildfire is present, and watch for combo win conditions in Mono Red Madness.
